title: Circular Hair Loss in Dogs: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Find out what causes circular hair loss in dogs, including ringworm and mites. Learn why this is a low-urgency issue and how photos help triage.
content_html:
What Are These Circular Patches?

Noticing circular patches of hair loss on your dog can be unsettling, but it is a common reason for veterinary visits. These bald spots, often called alopecia, are frequently caused by ringworm, which is a fungal infection rather than a literal worm. Other common causes include bacterial infections of the hair follicles (folliculitis), localized demodectic mange (mites), or even localized allergic reactions. While the hair loss itself isn't painful, the underlying cause might be itchy or irritating for your pet.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

The urgency level for circular hair loss is typically Low. While it requires a professional diagnosis and treatment plan, it is rarely a life-threatening emergency. You should schedule a routine appointment with your veterinarian within the next few days. However, if the skin appears raw, is bleeding, or if your dog is lethargic and refusing food, you should seek more immediate care.

How Photos Assist in Triage

When you contact a veterinary professional, providing a high-quality photo of the affected area is incredibly beneficial. A clear image allows us to see the shape of the patch, the condition of the surrounding skin, and whether there is any redness, scaling, or crusting. This visual information helps us determine the likelihood of a contagious condition like ringworm and helps us provide better guidance on how to manage the area until your appointment.

faqs.question: Can humans catch ringworm from their dogs?
faqs.answer: Yes, ringworm is a zoonotic disease, meaning it can be transmitted from animals to humans. It is important to wash your hands thoroughly after touching your dog and to keep them off shared furniture until a vet visit.

faqs.question: Should I apply any ointments at home?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid applying any creams or ointments until your vet has seen the dog. Topical treatments can interfere with diagnostic tests like fungal cultures or skin scrapings, making it harder to get an accurate diagnosis.

faqs.question: Will the hair grow back?
faqs.answer: In most cases, yes. Once the underlying infection or parasite is treated, the hair follicles usually recover and the fur will begin to grow back within a few weeks.
